title: ‘In transition’: choice and the children of New Zealand’s economic reforms
abstract: Abstract New Zealand's rapid emergence as a late‐modern, neo‐liberal society following 1984 led to a transformation in the institutional infrastructure for youth transitions from school to post‐school worlds. Our research focuses on the ways that young people born after 1984 craft identities in transition. We investigate their perspectives on transition in their last year of school, and the processes by which they make choices about post‐school destinations. In particular, we examine the extent to which the transitions they negotiate are shaped by the institutional infrastructure for transition established by policy. Expecting some degree of mismatch between the complexities of participants' lives and the linear transition process implicit in policy, we found instead a combination of traditional assumptions (that transition would be a straightforward, linear process) and late‐modern assumptions (about the construction of elective biographies through active choice). These combined to produce a particular perception of risk among participants. Acknowledgements This project is funded by the Royal Society of New Zealand Marsden Fund. The authors would like to thank Judith Sligo, Brigid Thompson and the peer researchers for research assistance. They also thank two anonymous referees for helpful comments. Notes 1. In contrast to highly developed apprenticeship systems in some European countries, this system did not institutionalise relationships between schools and workplaces. 2. Participants' names are pseudonyms. 3. Bursary (phased out in 2003) was a final‐year school qualification attracting a small grant for university. 4. Some selection bias may be at work here. We tried to minimise this by seeking a diverse range of participants from more than one school and more than one year in school. Nevertheless, it is possible that many of those who responded to our request for participants may have been young people with middle‐class cultural capital and hence likely to see university as a possible destination.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
